title: MVS IPL & System Operations
description: IPL the MVS system, start VTAM/TSO, manage JES2 queues, and perform a clean shutdown. Every command is documented with mental models explaining what it does and why.

steps.title: IPL the System
steps.screen:
Hercules Console:
> ipl 150

MVS Console (3270):
IEA101A SPECIFY SYSTEM PARAMETERS FOR RELEASE 03.8 NIP

Reply:
> r 0,clpa    (first IPL — rebuilds Link Pack Area)
> r 0,        (subsequent IPLs — accept defaults)

IFB010D SPECIFY IPL REASON
> r 0,u       (unplanned — not important for emulator)

$HASP426 SPECIFY OPTIONS
> r 1,format,noreq   (first IPL — format JES2 spool)
  or just press ENTER (subsequent IPLs)
steps.expected: Understand that IPL loads MVS from the MVSRES volume at device address 150. The CLPA option rebuilds the Link Pack Area in paging files. On subsequent IPLs you can just press ENTER to accept defaults.
steps.hint: IPL = Initial Program Load. Device 150 is a 3350 DASD containing the MVS System Residence volume (mvsres.3350).

commands_learned.what: Boot MVS from device address 150 (MVSRES volume). Like pressing the power button on a real mainframe — the CPU reads the boot record from track 0 of that DASD volume.
Reply to a system message. MVS assigns sequential reply IDs to messages needing a response. The ID tells MVS which question you're answering.
Rebuild the Link Pack Area — a shared memory region containing frequently used system modules. Required on first IPL; optional after that.
Tell JES2 to format its spool queues (first time only) and start without waiting for additional operator requests.

key_files: SYS1.PARMLIB(IEAPAK00) — Link Pack Area module list
SYS1.PARMLIB(COMMND00) — commands auto-executed at IPL (starts SETPFKEY, ZTIMER)
mvslog.txt — hardcopy console log (all messages captured here)

steps.title: Start VTAM & TSO
steps.screen:
MVS Console:
> s net

Messages:
IST020I VTAM INITIALIZATION COMPLETE
IST025I ... (TCAM not set up — ignore)
IKT007I VTSO IS READY FOR LOGON

TSO Terminal (port 3270, LU name 'tso'):
 IKJ56700A ENTER USERID -
> logon herc01
 IKJ56710I ENTER PASSWORD
> CUL8TR

Welcome message and TSO READY prompt appear.
steps.expected: VTAM manages the terminal network. The 's net' command starts the NET cataloged procedure which brings up VTAM. TSO starts automatically after VTAM initializes. Users connect via TN3270 on port 3270.
steps.hint: NET is a cataloged procedure in SYS1.PROCLIB. Up to 8 concurrent TSO sessions are supported (devices 400-407). IST025I messages about TCAM are safe to ignore.

commands_learned.what: Start VTAM via the NET cataloged procedure. 's' means 'start a cataloged procedure'. Cataloged procedures are pre-written JCL stored in SYS1.PROCLIB. VTAM manages all terminal connections.
Log on to a TSO session. TSO authenticates you and creates a personal address space. Your userid becomes your dataset name prefix.

steps.mental_model: Think of VTAM as the telephone switchboard — it routes terminal connections to the right application (TSO, CICS, etc.). TSO is the interactive shell — like a Unix login session. Each TSO user gets their own protected memory space.

steps.title: Submit Jobs via Socket Reader
steps.screen:
Hercules Console:
> sh ./submit.sh jcl/mvs00.jcl

Messages from Hercules:
HHC01024I Sending jcl/mvs00.jcl to 127.0.0.1 port 3505
HHC01090I Socket client 127.0.0.1 connected to 000C
HHC01091I Socket client 127.0.0.1 disconnected from 000C

MVS Console shows job messages:
$HASP100 MVS00    ON READER1
IEF403I MVS00 - STARTED
IEFACTRT ...
steps.expected: The socket reader at device 00C listens on port 3505. The submit script uses ncat to send JCL file contents to that port. JES2 picks up the job and schedules it for execution based on the CLASS= parameter.
steps.hint: The 'sh' prefix on the Hercules console passes the command to the host OS shell. You can also submit from a separate terminal window: ./submit.sh jcl/mvs00.jcl

commands_learned.what: 'sh' tells Hercules to run a host OS command. The submit script uses ncat to send JCL to port 3505 where the MVS socket reader is listening. This is the virtual equivalent of feeding punch cards into a card reader.
Mount a virtual tape. Device 100 is a 3420 tape drive. The .het file is a Hercules Emulated Tape image. This is like a computer operator physically mounting a tape reel.

steps.mental_model: In the real mainframe world, jobs were submitted on punch cards fed into a card reader. The socket reader emulates this — your JCL file is the 'card deck', ncat is the 'card reader', and port 3505 is where the reader is connected.

steps.title: JES2 Queue Management
steps.screen:
TSO READY:
> q

QUEUE COMMAND:
> ho

Held Output Queue:
 JOBNAME  JOBID    CLASS  LINES
 HELLO    JOB00012   X      3
 MVS01    JOB00008   X     45
 MVS02    JOB00009   X    120

Place S in front of a job to view output.
Place D in front to see SYSOUT DD list.
Place P in front to purge.

PF1 = Help   PF3 = Exit   PF7/PF8 = Scroll
steps.expected: QUEUE is a TSO application for viewing and managing JES2 output queues. The HO command shows held output. You can select, display, purge, and search through job output interactively.
steps.hint: Regularly purge old output to prevent the JES2 spool from filling up. ZTIMER auto-purges output older than 5 days. If the spool fills completely, the system will halt.

commands_learned.what: Launch QUEUE — the JES2 spool browser. Think of it as a visual job output manager where you can browse, search, and purge job output.
Display the Held Output queue — shows all jobs with output waiting to be viewed or printed.
Show status of a specific job — whether it's queued, running, or has output.

commands_learned.where: QUEUE — in front of a job line
commands_learned.what: S=Select (view output), D=Display SYSOUT DDs, P=Purge (delete output). Position cursor in front of the job and type the letter.
N=Next output dataset, F=Find text. Use these to navigate through multi-dataset job output.

steps.mental_model: JES2 is the traffic controller for batch jobs. It manages three phases: INPUT (reading JCL), EXECUTION (running the job via initiators), and OUTPUT (printing/holding results). QUEUE lets you inspect the OUTPUT phase.

steps.title: Display System Status
steps.screen:
MVS Console:
> d a,l

Active Address Spaces:
 JOBNAME  ASID  STATUS
 *MASTER*  001  ACTIVE
 JES2     002  ACTIVE
 NET      003  ACTIVE
 TSO      004  ACTIVE
 HMVS01   005  ACTIVE

> d u,dasd

DASD Status:
 UNIT ADDR  VOLSER  STATUS  DEVTYPE
 0150       MVSRES  ONLINE  3350
 0151       MVS000  ONLINE  3350
 0152       PAGE00  ONLINE  3350
 0153       SPOOL1  ONLINE  3350
 0180       PUB000  ONLINE  3380
 0190       PUB001  ONLINE  3390

> d pfk

PF Key Assignments:
 PF01 = D A,L       PF02 = D U,DASD
 PF03 = D R,L       ...
steps.expected: These display commands let you see what's running and what devices are available. D A,L shows all active address spaces (like 'ps' on Unix). D U,DASD shows all DASD volumes. D PFK shows PF key shortcuts.
steps.hint: Messages ending in 'A' require action (reply). Messages ending in 'D' are decision messages. Messages ending in 'I' are informational only.

commands_learned.what: Display all active address spaces — every running job, started task, and TSO user. Like 'ps aux' on Unix. ASID is the Address Space ID.
Display all DASD devices — address, volume serial, online/offline status, and device type. Essential for verifying volumes are mounted.
Display PF key assignments. PF keys are programmable shortcuts defined in SYS1.PARMLIB(SETPFK00).
Display all outstanding reply messages — shows every message waiting for an operator response. Use this when the system seems hung.
Display pending console messages. If messages scrolled off screen, this shows what's still waiting.

steps.title: Orderly Shutdown
steps.screen:
TSO Session:
READY
> logoff

MVS Console — shut down in this exact order:
1. > p tso           Stop TSO
2. > z net,quick     Stop VTAM
3. > $p jes2         Stop JES2
4. > z eod           End of day (SMF records)
5. > quiesce         Flush memory to DASD

Hercules Console:
6. > quit            Exit emulator
steps.expected: Always shut down in the correct order: logoff TSO sessions first, then stop TSO, VTAM, JES2, issue end-of-day, quiesce, and finally quit Hercules. This prevents data loss and corruption of system datasets.
steps.hint: NEVER kill Hercules without QUIESCE — data in memory buffers will be lost. After shutdown, back up the dasd directory.

commands_learned.what: Cleanly end your TSO session. ALWAYS logoff before closing the terminal — closing without logoff leaves the userid stuck.
'p' = stop a started task gracefully. TSO will terminate after all users are logged off.
Halt VTAM immediately. The QUICK option skips waiting for sessions to end gracefully (they should already be ended).
Stop JES2. The $ prefix marks it as a JES2 command. JES2 waits for active jobs to finish before terminating.
End of day — writes final SMF (System Management Facility) records and switches to the alternate SMF dataset.
Flush ALL memory buffers to DASD. System enters a hardware wait state. This is the critical step that prevents data loss.
Exit the Hercules emulator. Only safe AFTER quiesce.

steps.mental_model: The shutdown order matters because each layer depends on the one below it. TSO needs VTAM. VTAM needs JES2. JES2 needs MVS. Shutting down in reverse order ensures each layer cleans up before its dependencies disappear.
